args.body: Thanks for surfacing this. Based on the record for T-340: two hours were added to the timesheet after the approval cutoff, and while the camera log confirms the work was actually performed, the entry was backdated after a manager's overtime reminder. The correct path is to (1) leave the hours in place since the work occurred, (2) note in the timesheet that the entry was made post-cutoff, and (3) route the timing/process issue to the manager for a direct conversation with Maya. I am not adding any warning or performance-plan language to this reply, because no prior warning or repair plan is documented in this record. If one exists, please point me to it and I will incorporate it.

Facts on record: Maya Lewis added two hours to her timesheet after the approval cutoff. Camera log corroborates that the work was performed late as claimed. Entry was made after a manager reminded the team about overtime, so the addition is properly characterized as a backdated post-cutoff edit, not falsified hours. Recommended handling: keep the hours (work occurred), annotate the entry as a post-cutoff correction, and have the manager address timesheet timing with Maya directly. Note: requester referenced "warning and repair-plan facts" for Maya; no such warning or repair plan is present in T-340 or attached to this record. Nothing of that nature was added to the customer reply or this memo, to avoid asserting undocumented disciplinary history.
